questions.question: What happens when you pass a String to a function without a reference?
options: Ownership is transferred to the function
The original variable is still valid
questions.explanation: Non-Copy types like String are moved. The caller can no longer use the original variable.

questions.question: Types like i32, f64, and bool implement Copy and are copied instead of moved.
questions.explanation: Simple stack-only scalar types implement Copy. Assignment creates a bitwise copy.

questions.question: Fix this code by borrowing instead of moving:
questions.explanation: `&String` borrows without taking ownership, so `s1` remains valid.

questions.question: How many mutable references to the same data can exist in the same scope?
questions.explanation: Rust allows at most one mutable reference at a time to prevent data races.

questions.question: Keep both variables valid by creating a deep copy:
questions.explanation: clone() creates a deep copy of the heap data so both variables own independent copies.

questions.question: You can have an immutable and a mutable reference to the same data at the same time.
questions.explanation: Rust prevents this. You can have multiple immutable references OR one mutable reference, never both.
